CHAPTER 11 — ADVANCED EXCEL SPREADSHEETS: STATISTICAL AND DATA ANALYSIS

Start with the simplest case. You have a column of numbers — revenues from fifty customers who received campaign A. You

Chapter 11 — Advanced Excel Spreadsheets — The mean is the sum divided by the count. If ten customers generate revenues of 1,250, 890, 3,420, 450, 5,100, 620, 2,80
NARRATION

Start with the simplest case. You have a column of numbers , revenues from fifty customers who received campaign A. You want to understand what those numbers are doing. Summary statistics are the tools for this. The mean is the sum divided by the count. If ten customers generate revenues of 1,250, 890, 3,420, 450, 5,100, 620, 2,800, 1,950, 4,100, and 2,300, the mean is their sum (22,880) divided by ten, which is 2,288. Excel calculates this with =AVERAGE().

// SLIDE 02 — WHAT A PIVOT TABLE DOES

WHAT A PIVOT TABLE DOES.

A — 45,200
B — 38,600
Total — 83,800
NARRATION

A pivot table takes raw data with many rows and many columns and reorganizes it into a summary grid. The word "pivot" is apt: you rotate the data , change what is in the rows, what is in the columns , and see it from different angles without touching the raw data itself. Here is why you need one. The campaign data has 2,847 rows. Each row is one customer. The columns are customer ID, campaign (A or B), region (North, South, East, West), and revenue.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT REGRESSION TELLS YOU

WHAT REGRESSION TELLS YOU.

85%
of the variation in revenue — the remaining 15% is explained
→
5%
chance of seeing a relationship this strong if there were ac
NARRATION

Summary statistics describe one variable at a time. A pivot table shows how one variable groups by another. Regression asks a different question: does knowing X help you predict Y? If advertising spend goes up by ten thousand dollars, how much does revenue go up? Linear regression finds the equation of the form $y = a + bx$ that best fits a scatter of points.

// SLIDE 04 — WORKING BACKWARD: GOAL SEEK

WORKING BACKWARD: GOAL SEEK

Sometimes you know where you want to end up and need to find the input. Revenue must be 300,000 next quarter. Advertisin

Working Backward: Goal Seek — You could solve the equation by hand: $300,000 = 100,000 + 2.5 \times \text{spend}$, so spend = 80,000. Excel's Goal See
NARRATION

Sometimes you know where you want to end up and need to find the input. Revenue must be 300,000 next quarter. Advertising spend is the lever. The regression equation gives you the relationship. What spend is required? You could solve the equation by hand: $300,000 = 100,000 + 2.5 \times \text{spend}$, so spend = 80,000. Excel's Goal Seek does this automatically, and it is useful when the equation is embedded in a complex model where solving by hand is not straightforward.

// SLIDE 05 — THE THREE TOOLS AS A WORKFLOW

THE THREE TOOLS AS A WORKFLOW.

First question: what is the data doing?→Second question: how does the data vary across groups?→Third question: does one variable predict another?
NARRATION

Summary statistics, pivot tables, and regression are not three separate things to memorize. They are three questions asked in sequence. First question: what is the data doing? Summary statistics answer this. Mean, median, and standard deviation describe the central tendency and spread of each variable individually. Before you do anything else, understand what you have. If the mean and median are far apart, the distribution is skewed and the mean will mislead you.

// SLIDE 06 — EXERCISES

EXERCISES

40%
higher than Q2
→
8.5%
difference — campaign A is clearly better
NARRATION

11.1 (Mean vs. median , predict before you calculate.) You have seven customer revenues: 500, 800, 1,200, 900, 600, 700, 15,000. Without calculating, predict: will the mean be higher than, lower than, or close to the median? Then calculate both. Was your prediction correct? Explain in one sentence why the mean and median diverged or did not.

// SLIDE 07 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Ask it to explain in plain language what the Cramér–Rao bound says about how good any estimator can possibly bewithout using formulas
Ask"Rao studied under R. A. Fisher at Cambridge and lived to 102. What did he carry forward from Fisher's framework, and wh
Add the constraint"Answer using one worked example of a real-world analysis where the Cramér–Rao bound matters — what an analyst loses by
NARRATION

The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. C. R. Rao was working on the Cramér, Rao bound, the Rao, Blackwell theorem, and decades of foundational statistical theory , the math behind the confidence intervals every regression and A/B test silently relies on decades before most people had heard of statistical analysis in spreadsheets. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

// SLIDE 08 — PROMPTS

PROMPTS

NARRATION

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML chart using a quantitative y-axis with a zero baseline, 4-6 labeled categories on the x-axis, direct value labels above marks, one primary red series and neutral supporting marks. The figure should represent: Two datasets shown as dot plots , left dataset: symmetric distribution where mean and median are nearly identical, centered around 2,200; right dataset: skewed distribution with one outlier at 50,000, showing mean pulled far right to ~6,700 while median stays near 2,100 , student should see visually why mean and

WHAT SUMMARY STATISTICS ACTUALLY SAY.

NARRATION

Start with the simplest case. You have a column of numbers , revenues from fifty customers who received campaign A. You want to understand what those numbers are doing. Summary statistics are the tools for this. The mean is the sum divided by the count. If ten customers generate revenues of 1,250, 890, 3,420, 450, 5,100, 620, 2,800, 1,950, 4,100, and 2,300, the mean is their sum (22,880) divided by ten, which is 2,288.
